title: I See Music in Colors – Young Children (Ages 6–8)

hunks.text: Some people see COLORS when they hear MUSIC. This is called synesthesia. It is a special way their BRAIN works. When you listen to a SONG, most people just hear sounds. But some people also see COLORS in their mind. Red might be loud and fast. Blue might be soft and slow. It is like their ears and eyes work together.

questions.question: What do some people see when they hear music?
Imagine you hear a very loud, happy song. What colors do you think you might see?
What is the special name for seeing colors when you hear music?
Name something that the text says happens with ears and eyes together.
questions.answer: THEY WORK TOGETHER / THEY BOTH HELP YOU EXPERIENCE MUSIC

hunks.text: Your BRAIN is like a superpower machine. It helps you do everything. It helps you think, move, remember, and feel. When you listen to music, your brain is very busy. It gets signals from your ears. Your brain notices if the music is FAST or SLOW. It notices if it is LOUD or SOFT. For people with synesthesia, their brain does something extra. It also sends COLORS to their mind.

questions.question: What does your brain help you do?
What part of your body helps you listen to music?
Tell me two ways your brain notices music.
questions.answer: FAST OR SLOW / LOUD OR SOFT

questions.question: What is something special you think your brain can do?

hunks.text: Different NOTES in music make different COLORS for synesthetes. A synesthete is a person who sees colors when they hear music. The NOTE C might be RED. The NOTE E might be YELLOW. The NOTE G might be GREEN. Each person sees different colors. Your best friend might see blue when you see red for the same note. There is no wrong way to see the colors. Everyone's BRAIN is unique and special.

questions.question: What is a person called who sees colors when they hear music?
Close your eyes and think about the color yellow. What kind of music sound might go with that color?
Name one thing the text says about how different people see colors.
questions.answer: EACH PERSON SEES DIFFERENT COLORS / YOUR FRIEND MIGHT SEE BLUE WHEN YOU SEE RED / THERE IS NO WRONG WAY

questions.question: What makes everyone's brain special?

hunks.text: Not everyone has synesthesia. Most people just hear music and do not see colors. That is totally okay. Your EARS are wonderful just the way they are. Some people with synesthesia say it helps them remember music. They hear a song and remember the colors they see. This helps them learn music faster. Some synesthetes are musicians and composers. They use their color gift to make beautiful music.

questions.question: Do most people see colors when they hear music?
Name one way synesthesia helps people.
questions.answer: HELPS THEM REMEMBER MUSIC / HELPS THEM LEARN MUSIC FASTER / HELPS THEM MAKE BEAUTIFUL MUSIC

questions.question: What do you think is special about your ears?
Who can be musicians with the color gift?

hunks.text: Many famous musicians and artists have had synesthesia. Stevie Wonder is a musician who is blind but hears music in colors. Franz Liszt was a piano player from long ago. He said things like 'play me the color blue.' Duke Ellington wrote jazz music. He talked about seeing colors when he made his music. These artists used their special brains to create wonderful music that people still love today.

questions.question: Who is a musician that hears music in colors?
questions.answer: STEVIE WONDER / FRANZ LISZT / DUKE ELLINGTON

questions.question: What instrument do many piano players play?
Name something one of these musicians did.
questions.answer: STEVIE WONDER HEARS MUSIC IN COLORS / FRANZ LISZT PLAYED PIANO / DUKE ELLINGTON WROTE JAZZ MUSIC / THEY CREATED WONDERFUL MUSIC

questions.question: If you could create music like these artists, what would you make?

hunks.text: You can make your own color music game. Pick a SONG you like. Listen to it carefully. What colors do you see in your mind? You might see RED, BLUE, YELLOW, GREEN, or ORANGE. Draw or paint the colors you imagine. You can make a COLOR picture of your song. There is no right or wrong answer. Your picture shows how the music makes you feel and what your amazing brain creates.

questions.question: Imagine listening to a happy, bouncy song. What colors might you paint?
What can you do with the colors you imagine from music?
Name one color that was mentioned you could imagine.
What is your favorite song to listen to?

hunks.text: Scientists study synesthesia to learn how our brains work. They do tests and ask synesthetes questions. They want to know why some brains see colors and other brains do not. You might have synesthesia, or you might be born without it. Either way, your BRAIN is amazing. Scientists think synesthesia might run in families. Sometimes a parent has it and their child has it too. But we still have much to learn.

questions.question: Who studies how synesthesia works?
Name one thing scientists want to know about synesthesia.
questions.answer: WHY SOME BRAINS SEE COLORS / WHY OTHER BRAINS DO NOT / HOW OUR BRAINS WORK

questions.question: Can a parent pass something to their child?
What question would you ask a scientist about color and music?

hunks.text: Your SENSES are gifts. You have ears to hear, eyes to see, a nose to smell, a tongue to taste, and skin to feel. All of these senses help you know the world. Synesthetes have a mix of senses that work together in a special way. Maybe you see colors in music. Maybe you taste colors. Maybe you hear shapes. Or maybe your senses work just like most people's. There is no better or worse way. Every brain and every person is wonderfully different and beautiful.

questions.question: Name one sense you have.
What do all your senses help you do?
Think about your favorite food. What do you see, smell, and taste?
What makes you think your brain is special and beautiful?

citations: Cytowic, R. E., & Eagleman, D. M. (2009). Wednesday is Indigo Blue: Discovering the Brain of Synesthesia. MIT Press.
Hubbard, E. M., Arman, A. C., Ramachandran, V. S., & Boynton, G. M. (2005). Individual differences among grapheme–color synesthetes: Brain–behavior correlations. Neuron, 45(6), 975–985.
Simner, J., & Hubbard, E. M. (Eds.). (2013). The Oxford Handbook of Synesthesia. Oxford University Press.
